revision.text:
[[de:Jōdo-shū]][[en:Jōdo-shū]][[es:Jōdo shū]][[fa:فرقه جودو]][[fr:Jōdo shū]][[hu:Dzsódo]][[ja:浄土宗]][[no:Jōdo-shū]][[pl:Jōdo-shū]][[ro:Jōdo-shū]][[ru:Дзёдо-сю]][[sl:Jōdo]][[sr:Школа чисте земље]][[sv:Jodoshu]][[tr:Jōdo-shū]][[uk:Секта Дзьодо]][[wuu:淨土宗]][[zh:净土宗 (日本)]] [[de:Jōdo-shū]][[en:Jōdo-shū]][[es:Jōdo shū]][[fa:فرقه جودو]][[fr:Jōdo shū]][[hu:Dzsódo]][[ja:浄土宗]][[no:Jōdo-shū]][[pl:Jōdo-shū]][[ro:Jōdo-shū]][[ru:Дзёдо-сю]][[sl:Jōdo]][[sr:Школа чисте земље]][[sv:Jodoshu]][[tr:Jōdo-shū]][[uk:Секта Дзьодо]][[wuu:淨土宗]][[zh:净土宗 (日本)]] [[de:Jōdo-shū]][[en:Jōdo-shū]][[es:Jōdo shū]][[fa:فرقه جودو]][[fr:Jōdo shū]][[hu:Dzsódo]][[ja:浄土宗]][[no:Jōdo-shū]][[pl:Jōdo-shū]][[ro:Jōdo-shū]][[ru:Дзёдо-сю]][[sl:Jōdo]][[sr:Школа чисте земље]][[sv:Jodoshu]][[tr:Jōdo-shū]][[uk:Секта Дзьодо]][[wuu:淨土宗]][[zh:净土宗 (日本)]] [[de:Jōdo-shū]][[en:Jōdo-shū]][[es:Jōdo shū]][[fa:فرقه جودو]][[fr:Jōdo shū]][[hu:Dzsódo]][[ja:浄土宗]][[no:Jōdo-shū]][[pl:Jōdo-shū]][[ro:Jōdo-shū]][[ru:Дзёдо-сю]][[sl:Jōdo]][[sr:Школа чисте земље]][[sv:Jodoshu]][[tr:Jōdo-shū]][[uk:Секта Дзьодо]][[wuu:淨土宗]][[zh:净土宗 (日本)]] {{short description|Branch of Pure Land Buddhism widely practiced in Japan}} {{other uses|Jodo (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religion|icon=月形杏葉（つきがた ぎょうよう）.png|icon_width=90px|name=Jōdo-shū|native_name=浄土宗|image=Chion-in (Mieido).jpg|caption=[[Chion-in]], Grand Head Temple of the {{ill|Chinzei|en|Chinzei|qid=Q2963795|lt=Chinzei|1=Chinzei}} Branch of Jōdo-shū|main_classification={{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}}|scripture=The Three Pure Land Sutras and the {{ill|Senchakushū|en|Senchakushū|qid=Q7450101|lt=Senchakushū|1=Senchakushū}}|division1={{ill|Chinzei|en|Chinzei|qid=Q2963795|lt=Chinzei|1=Chinzei}}, {{ill|Seizan|en|Seizan|qid=Q7447036|lt=Seizan|1=Seizan}}|liturgy=Jōdo-shū Otsutome|headquarters=[[Chion-in]] (Chinzei), {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|en|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|qid=Q2380134|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji}} (Seizan)|founder=[[:en:Honen Shonin|Honen Shonin]]|founded_date=1175|founded_place={{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}|separated_from={{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}}|churches=7,000{{cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/BB/0005/BB00050L001.pdf|title=The originator of Japanese Pure Land Buddhism, St.Honen, His Life and Thought|publisher=Bukkyo University}}|members=Over 6,000,000{{cite web|url=https://osoushiki-plaza.com/library/data/data131.html|title=日本の主な宗教宗派|publisher=文化庁|date=1998}}|ministers=9,500{{cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/BB/0005/BB00050L001.pdf|title=The originator of Japanese Pure Land Buddhism, St.Honen, His Life and Thought|publisher=Bukkyo University}}|website={{plainlist| * [https://jodo.or.jp/ Official Japanese Website of Jōdo-shū] * [https://jodo.or.jp/en/ Official English Website of Jōdo-shū] }}}}{{buddhism}}{{nihongo|'''Jōdo-shū'''|浄土宗||"The Pure Land School"}}, also known as '''Jōdo Buddhism''', is a branch of {{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}} derived from the teachings of the Japanese ex-{{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} monk {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}}. It was established in 1175 and is the most widely practiced branch of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}}, along with [[Jōdo Shinshū]]. In the general classification of Buddhism in Japan, the Jōdo-shū, the [[:en:Jōdo Shinshu|Jōdo Shinshu]], the {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|qid=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu|1=Ji-shu}} and the [[Yuzu Nembutsu|Yuzu Nembutsu shu]] are collectively classified into the lineage of Jōdo Buddhism. (Jōdo kei, 浄土系)[https://web.archive.org/web/20220121012259/https://bookclub.kodansha.co.jp/product?item=0000326003 詳説 日本仏教13宗派がわかる本.] {{ill|Kodansha|en|Kodansha|qid=Q726081|lt=Kodansha|1=Kodansha}}.[https://web.archive.org/web/20210522070837/https://kan-etsu-seien.com/publics/index/334/ 宗派について.] Kanetsu Seien. == History == === The Founder: Hōnen === {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} (法然) was born in 1133, the son of Uruma no Tokikuni of a local ruling family in {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|qid=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province|1=Mimasaka Province}}.[https://web.archive.org/web/20220325164324/https://honen850.jp/jodo/honen-1/ 浄土宗西山禅林寺派宗祖法然上人立教開宗850th year 記念サイト.] honen850.jp Hōnen was originally named Seishimaru after the [[mahāsattva]] Seishi (Sanskrit {{ill|Mahasthamaprapta|en|Mahasthamaprapta|qid=Q1465683|lt=Mahāsthāmaprāpta|1=Mahasthamaprapta}}). After a rival official assassinated his father in 1141, Hōnen was initiated into his uncle's monastery at the age of 9. From then on, Hōnen lived his life as a monk and eventually studied at the famous monastery of {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}. Hōnen was well respected for his knowledge and for his adherence to the [[:en:Five Precepts|Five Precepts]], but in time, Hōnen became dissatisfied with the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} teachings he learned at Mount Hiei. Influenced by the writings of {{ill|Shandao|en|Shandao|qid=Q1013023|lt=Shandao|1=Shandao}}, Hōnen devoted himself solely to [[Amitābha]] as expressed through the practice of {{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=nembutsu|1=Nianfo}}. In time, Hōnen gathered disciples from all walks of life, and developed a large following, notably women, who had been excluded from serious Buddhist practice up to this point. This included fishermen, prostitutes{{cite web|title=Nyorai-in in Settsu|url=http://www.jsri.jp/English/Jodoshu/hymns/4nyorai.html|access-date = 2008-11-23}} and fortune tellers. Hōnen also distinguished himself by not discriminating against women who were menstruating, thought at the time to be unclean. Some followers more strictly followed Buddhist {{ill|Śīla|en|Śīla|qid=Q1047844|lt=conduct|1=Śīla}}, whereas others assumed they were saved by [[Amitābha|Amida]] Buddha and behaved recklessly, earning criticism from established monastic communities such as [[:en:Enryakuji|Enryakuji]] and [[:en:Kofukuji|Kofukuji]]. To counter these criticisms, Honen insisted his followers sign the '''Seven Article Pledge''', or {{nihongo|''shichikajō seikai''|七箇条制戒||}} in 1204, pledging not to disparage other sects, and to uphold the Buddha-Dharma. One-hundred and sixty-three followers, including Honen's chief disciples, signed the pledge.{{cite book | last1 = Jones | first1 = Charles B. | title = Pure Land: History, Tradition, and Practice (Buddhist Foundations) | publisher = Shambhala | year = 2021 |isbn=978-1-61180-890-2 | pages=123–135}} === Protest and Banishment === The monastic communities were satisfied for a time, until in 1207, when Kofukuji temple made another petition to the Emperor. Meanwhile, two of Honen's disciples, Jūren (住蓮) and Anraku-bō (安楽房), were caught proselytizing to some of the ladies in waiting of [[:en:Emperor Go-toba|Emperor Go-toba]], who had then decided to take tonsure and leave the service of the Emperor. In anger, the Emperor decreed that Honen and several followers be defrocked and sent into exile, while Jūren and Anraku-bō were executed. This is recorded in the [[:en:Tannisho|Tannisho]] of {{ill|Shinran|en|Shinran|qid=Q512934|lt=Shinran|1=Shinran}}'s disciple, Yuien-bō, as follows:{{cite web|title=TANNISHO: A Record in Lament of Divergences| language=English| url=http://web.mit.edu/stclair/www/tannisho-all.html| accessdate = 2023-06-27}} {{blockquote|Master Honen and seven of his disciples were exiled, and four other disciples were executed. The Master was banished to a place called Hata in Tosa province and, stripped of ordination, given a secular name: the male Fujii no Motohiko; he was seventy-six years old. Shinran was exiled to Echigo province. His secular name was Fujii no Yoshizane, he was thirty-five. [Among the others exiled:] Jomon-bo, to Bingo province; Chosai Zenko-bo, to Hoki province; Kokaku-bo, to Izu province; Gyoku Hohon-bo, to Sado province. It was also determined that Kosai Jokaku-bo and Zenne-bo both receive banishment, but the former abbot of Mudo-ji temple took them under his custody.}} Jūren and Anraku-bō were executed along with a few other disciples. Eventually, Hōnen was pardoned and returned to Kyoto in 1211, but died soon after in 1212, just two days after giving his final testament, the ''{{ill|One-Sheet Document|en|One-Sheet Document|qid=Q11352846|lt=One-Sheet Document|1=One-Sheet Document}}'' to disciple Genchi. === Early Years After Hōnen === The remaining disciples in the {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=capitol|1=Heian-kyō}} collected Honen's writings and erected a mausoleum, while doctrinal disagreements between Honen's disciples Ryūkan, Chōsai and Shōkú escalated. When Hōnen's work, the Senchakushu, began to circulate in 1227, this enraged the monastic community on [[:en:Enryakuji|Enryakuji]], and a force of [[sōhei]] warriors were sent to raid the tomb of Hōnen. This is known as the '''Karoku Persecution''' (''karoku no hōgan'' 嘉禄の法難) of 1227. Honen's body and copies of the ''Senchakushu'' were relocated thanks to advance warning, but other writings of Honen's were destroyed, and more disciples were exiled. === Remote Disciples and Doctrines === Many, though not all, of the disciples of Honen were exiled to remote provinces in either 1207 by order of [[:en:Emperor Go-toba|Emperor Go-toba]], or later in 1227. Each established a local community in their respective provinces and with nuances in the teachings ("''gi''", 義), but only a two lineages survive today: the '''Chinzei-ha''' branch and the '''Seizan-ha''' branch (with 3 sub-branches). Other offshoots such as [[:en:Jodo Shinshu|Jodo Shinshu]] and the [[:en:Ji-shū|Ji-shū]] sects are considered different enough to be separate from Jodo-shū. {{chart top|Jodo-Shu Lineage Chart{{cite book | last1 = 服部 | first1 = 淳一 | title = 日本人として心が豊かになる仏事とおつとめ 浄土宗 単行本 | publisher = 青志社 | year = 2008 |isbn=978-4-903853-24-6 | pages=36–38}}}} {{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Jodo-shu lineage Chart}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |HONEN| | |HONEN='''[[:en:Honen|Hōnen]]'''
(法然)|}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |!| | |}} {{Tree chart| |,|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|v|-|-|^|v|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|.| | | | |}} {{Tree chart|TANKU||GENCHI||BENCHO||SHOKU||CHOSAI||KOSAI||RYUKAN||SHINRAN||SHINKU|TANKU=Tankū
(湛空)|GENCHI=Genchi
(源智)|BENCHO='''{{ill|Benchō|en|Benchō|qid=Q4886957|lt=Benchō|1=Benchō}}'''
(弁長)|SHOKU='''{{ill|Shōkū|en|Shōkū|qid=Q7506163|lt=Shōkū|1=Shōkū}}'''
(証空)|SHINRAN='''{{ill|Shinran|en|Shinran|qid=Q512934|lt=Shinran|1=Shinran}}'''
(親鸞)|CHOSAI=Chōsai
(長西)|KOSAI={{ill|Kōsai|en|Kōsai|qid=Q6455274|lt=Kōsai|1=Kōsai}}
(幸西)|RYUKAN=Ryūkan
(隆寛)|SHINKU=Shinkū
(信空)}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |:| | | |!| | | |)|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|v|-|-|-|.| | | |:| | |}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |:| | |RYOCHU| |JOON| |||||ENKU||SHOTATSU|||:||RYOCHU='''Ryōchū'''
(良忠)|JOON='''Jō-on'''
(浄音)|ENKU='''Enkū'''
(円空)|SHOTATSU=Shōtatsu
(聖達)||}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |:| | | |!| | | |)|-|-|-|.| | | |!| | ||!| | | |:| |}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |L|~|~|CHINZEI| |ZENRINJI||KOMYOJI||FUKAKUSA||IPPEN|||:||CHINZEI='''Chinzei branch'''|ZENRINJI='''Seizan''',
Zenrinji branch|KOMYOJI='''Seizan''',
Komyoji branch|FUKAKUSA='''Seizan''',
Fukakusa branch|IPPEN=[[Ippen]]
(一編)}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | | | | |!| | | |||||||||||||!| | | |:| |}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | | | |SHOGEI| || |||||||||||JISHU||SHINSHU||SHOGEI='''{{ill|Shōgei|en|Shōgei|qid=Q11610932|lt=Shōgei|1=Shōgei}}'''
(聖冏)|||SHOTATSU=Shōtatsu
(聖達)|JISHU=[[:en:Ji-shū|Ji-shū]] sect|SHINSHU='''[[:en:Jodo Shinshu|Jodo Shinshu]] sect'''||}} {{Tree chart/end}} {{chart bottom}} The largest branch of Jōdo-shū, the {{nihongo|'''Chinzei-ha'''|鎮西派||"The Chinzei Branch"}}, named after the district of Chinzei in [[:en:Kita-Kyushu|Kita-Kyushu]], was originally established in the hometown of disciple, {{ill|Benchō|en|Benchō|qid=Q4886957|lt=Benchō|1=Benchō}}, who had been exiled in 1207, but grew through subsequent disciples. The rival {{nihongo|'''Seizan-ha'''|西山派||"The Seizan Branch"}} and Kuhon-ji branches grew around the capitol of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, as Shōkū and Chōsai were among the few major disciples who were not exiled. Finally, Shinran, another disciple established communities in {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|qid=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province|1=Echigo Province}}, but the sect gradually differed enough from others to be considered a separate sect, [[:en:Jodo Shinshu|Jodo Shinshu]]. Other disciples of note: * '''Genchi''', Honen's disciple and personal attendant who stayed in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. Later, met Ryōchū in 1248, and agreed to merge with the Chinzei branch after reaching an accord.{{cite book|title=Traversing the Pure Land Path: A Lifetime of Encounters with Honen Shonin|year=2005|publisher=Jodo Shu Press|isbn=4-88363-342-X|pages=89–94,124–131,150–153}} Was Hōnen's witness when he dictated the {{ill|One-Sheet Document|en|One-Sheet Document|qid=Q11352846|lt=One-Sheet Document|1=One-Sheet Document}}, his final testament. * '''Shinkū''', helped to establish Hōnen's mausoleum, and later protect its relics during the Persecution of 1227. * '''Ryūkan''', one of Honen's more elderly disciples, emphasized the efficacy of the nembutsu as practice and encouraged its frequent recitation, leading to his teachings being called the "many callings school" or ''tanen-gi'' (多念義). He was exiled to eastern Japan in 1227, where he died en route. * '''{{ill|Kōsai|en|Kōsai|qid=Q6455274|lt=Kōsai|1=Kōsai}}''', a disciple who advocated that a single recitation of the nembutsu was all that was necessary. His doctrine of "once-calling" or ''ichinen-gi'' (一念義) provided considerable controversy, and Honen eventually disavowed Kōsai and his teachings. He was later exiled to the island of [[Shikoku]]. * '''Chōsai''', the last of Hōnen's direct disciples. Where Honen and other Pure Land followers focused on the 18th, or [[:en:Primal Vow|Primal Vow]], of [[Amitābha|Amida]] Buddha, Chõsai felt that the 20th Vow of the [[:en:Longer Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra|Immeasurable Life Sutra]] also applied, and thus taught that other practices in Buddhism would lead to birth in the Pure Land. This was the {{nihongo|''shogyō hongan''|諸行本願||"Primal Vow for Miscellaneous Practices"}} teaching. Founder of the ''Kuhonji-gi'' branch (九品寺義), based around Kuhonji temple in Kyoto. * '''[[:en:Kujō Kanezane|Kujō Kanezane]]''', an influential of the [[:en:Fujiwara Clan|Fujiwara Clan]], he helped defend and protect Honen from some political persecution. A number of letters and poetry exchanged Honen and Kanezane have been preserved. * '''Awanosuke''' (阿波介), the [[Onmyōji|fortune-teller]], is credited with the double-stranded {{ill|Buddhist prayer beads|en|Buddhist prayer beads|qid=Q761901|lt=Buddhist prayer beads|1=Buddhist prayer beads}} used in Jōdo-shū, though he did not establish a branch of his own. === Reintegration === A generation later, Bencho's disciple, Ryōchū, became his disciple for a year, and then spread Benchō's and Hōnen's teachings throughout Japan before reaching the new capital at {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}. Ryōchū helped to legitimize the "Chinzei branch" of Jōdo Shū as the mainstream one, and is credited as the 3rd Patriarch accordingly. He also referred to Benchō, his teacher, as the 2nd Patriarch after Hōnen. Ryōchū also met with Renjaku-bo, whose own teacher Genchi, had been another disciple of Hōnen. Renjaku-bo felt that Genchi and Benchō had been in complete agreement, so he willingly united his lineage with Ryōchū's, helping to further increase its standing.{{cite book|title=Traversing the Pure Land Path: A Lifetime of Encounters with Hōnen Shonin|year=2005|publisher=Jodo Shu Press|isbn=4-88363-342-X|pages=152–153}} Jōdo Shū through the Chinzei lineage continued to develop until the 8th Patriarch, {{ill|Shōgei|en|Shōgei|qid=Q11610932|lt=Shōgei|1=Shōgei}} (聖冏, 1341-1420) who formalized the training of priests (rather than training under Tendai or {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}} lineages), thus formally establishing it as an independent sect. === Edo Period === In 1590, during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, Jōdo-shū was officially patronized by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}},{{Cite web |title=JODO SHU English |url=http://www.jodo.org/about_js/history.html |access-date=2023-04-24 |website=www.jodo.org}} leading to an era of great prosperity for the sect that lasted throughout the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} and beyond. {{ill|Zōjō-ji|en|Zōjō-ji|qid=Q249139|lt=Zōjō-ji|1=Zōjō-ji}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}, previously a [[Shingon]] sect temple converted to a Jōdo-shū temple, became the family temple of the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the same year, receiving considerable patronage.{{Cite web |title=大本山 増上寺 |url=https://www.zojoji.or.jp/ |access-date=2023-04-24 |website=www.zojoji.or.jp |language=ja}} Several {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}s of the Tokugawa family are still interred in mausoleums at Zōjō-ji, though Ieyasu himself is interred at the {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} shrine. === Meiji Period === [[File:Fukuda Gyōkai.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Fukuda Gyōkai|en|Fukuda Gyōkai|qid=Q89448947|lt=Fukuda Gyōkai|1=Fukuda Gyōkai}}, an important Jōdo sect reformer during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji Period|1=Meiji era}}.]] The [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868 marked a tumultuous period for Japanese Buddhism. The separation of Shinto and Buddhism unintentionally triggered the [[haibutsu kishaku]], a nationwide movement targeting Buddhist institutions, in which temples were demolished, their properties revoked, and their monks forcibly defrocked.[[John Breen (scholar)|John Breen]] (July 2000). [[Mark Teeuwen]] (ed.). ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami''. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. p. 230. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}}. {{OCLC|43487317}} Jōdo-shū, having formerly received considerable patronage from the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], now embarked on a period of internal reform in which several monks emerged as important reformers. Among these reformers was [[Tetsujō Ukai]] (養鸕徹定), a Buddhist historian and the head priest of [[Chion-in]] from 1885 to 1887. He became a staunch defender of Buddhism during this period, opposing both the attempts to eradicate Buddhism in Japan and the rise of Western criticism aimed at the religion. He also emphasized the importance of Buddhism in Japanese history and culture, underscoring its contributions to the nation's history.{{Cite web |title=養鸕徹定 |url=http://jodoshuzensho.jp/daijiten |access-date=2024-01-23 |website=WEB版新纂浄土宗大辞典 |language=ja}} Another important figure within the reform movement was {{ill|Fukuda Gyōkai|en|Fukuda Gyōkai|qid=Q89448947|lt=Fukuda Gyōkai|1=Fukuda Gyōkai}} (福田行誡), a Buddhist scholar, poet, and the head priest of Chion-in from 1887 to 1888.{{Cite web |last=知恩院 |first=浄土宗総本山 |title=福田行誡上人の足跡を訪ねて｜浄土宗総本山 知恩院 |url=https://www.chion-in.or.jp/kacho/2077/ |access-date=2024-01-23 |website=浄土宗総本山 知恩院 |language=ja}} He promoted both the preservation of Buddhist traditions and the modernization of social welfare systems based on {{ill|Buddhist philosophy|en|Buddhist philosophy|qid=Q1001079|lt=Buddhist philosophy|1=Buddhist philosophy}}. He also promoted the creation of the League of United Buddhist Sects (諸宗同徳会盟), which united several Buddhist sects to follow the goals of modernization and preventing {{ill|Christianity in Japan|en|Christianity in Japan|qid=Q427945|lt=Christian|1=Christianity in Japan}} influences on Japan.{{Cite web |title=諸宗同徳会盟 |url=http://jodoshuzensho.jp/daijiten |access-date=2024-01-22 |website=WEB版新纂浄土宗大辞典 |language=ja}} === Shōwa Period === Before and during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, Jōdo-shū, along with other Buddhist sects, faced pressure to endorse the actions of {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Imperial Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} and its policy of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shintō|1=State Shinto}}. Despite the protests of certain members of the sect, the Jōdo-shū organization nevertheless complied with the policy of State Shintō. This included building several temples within Japan’s {{ill|en:List of territories acquired by the Empire of Japan|en|en:List of territories acquired by the Empire of Japan|qid=Q278331|lt=overseas colonies|1=List of territories acquired by the Empire of Japan}} (all of which would later be destroyed in the years following the war) and providing memorial services to deceased soldiers as well as relief for their families. The school subsequently apologized for its wartime actions alongside the other Buddhist sects of Japan.Victoria, Brian (1992). ZEN AT WAR (2nd ed.). Oxford, England: Rowman & Littlefield Publishers (published 2006). After the Second World War, during the 1940s and early 1950s, several temples broke off from the main Chinzei Branch of Jōdo-shū, forming their own independent sects. However, this period of fragmentation proved to be relatively short-lived. In January 1961, on the 750th anniversary of Hōnen’s death, the majority of the breakaway sects of Jōdo-shū merged back into the primary {{ill|Chinzei|en|Chinzei|qid=Q2963795|lt=Chinzei|1=Chinzei}} branch, which remains the largest branch of Jōdo-shū in the modern day. == Geographic Distribution == [[File:Honen shonin eden - Public preach.jpg|thumb|An artistic depiction of {{ill|Hōnen|en|Hōnen|qid=Q507980|lt=Hōnen|1=Hōnen}} publicly preaching.]] Although Jōdo-shū is mainly found in Japan, a sizable Jōdo-shū community exists in {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|qid=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}} as well as a few temples in the continental {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}} and {{ill|Brazil|en|Brazil|qid=Q155|lt=Brazil|1=Brazil}}. The first Jōdo-shū temple built in Hawai'i was the Hāmākua Bukkyo Kaido, constructed in 1896 under the supervision of Reverend Gakuo Okabe.{{Cite web |last=HistoricHawaii |title=Hāmākua Jodo Mission: Past, Present and Future |url=https://historichawaii.org/2021/08/12/hamakua-jodo-mission-past-present-and-future/ |access-date=2023-05-31 |website=Historic Hawaii Foundation |language=en-US}}{{Cite web |date=2019-12-14 |title=Our History |url=https://hamakuajodomission.org/our-history/ |access-date=2023-05-31 |website=Hamakua Jodo Mission |language=en}} The Jodo Shu North America Buddhist Missions was the first Jōdo-shū temple to be built in mainland America in 1936 in {{ill|Los Angeles|en|Los Angeles|qid=Q65|lt=Los Angeles, California|1=Los Angeles}}.{{Cite web |title=JODO SHU English |url=http://www.jodo.org/js_inter/temples_us01.html |access-date=2023-05-31 |website=www.jodo.org}}{{Cite web |title=Jodo Shu North America Buddhist Missions {{!}} |url=https://english.jodoshuna.org/ |access-date=2023-05-31 |website=english.jodoshuna.org}} == Doctrine == {{ill|Pure Land|en|Pure Land|qid=Q732344|lt=Pure Land|1=Pure Land}} Buddhist teachings had been prevalent in Japan for centuries, particularly in the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect through {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|qid=Q732282|lt=Ennin|1=Ennin}}, {{ill|Genshin|en|Genshin|qid=Q1009263|lt=Genshin|1=Genshin}}, and others, but what distinguished Honen's teaching was the notion {{nihongo|'''senju nembutsu'''|専修念仏||"exclusive nembutsu"}}, whereby the only true means of achieving rebirth in the Pure Land was through reciting the {{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Nianfo|1=Nianfo}}. In particular, Honen argued that it was through [[Amitābha|Amida]] Buddha's merit and compassion that one achieved rebirth, and since the nembutsu was explicitly called out in the [[:en:Longer Sukhāvatīvyūha Sūtra|Immeasurable Life Sutra]] in the 18th Vow (also known as the [[:en:Primal Vow|Primal Vow]]), it was the only practice that would work, especially in the latter age of ''[[:en:Three Ages of Buddhism|Mappō]]'', when people could no longer effectively put the teachings of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] into practice anymore. Other practices would neither add nor detract from Amida Buddha's power. Toward the end of the Immeasurable Life Sutra is the following passage: {{blockquote|The Buddha further said, "I have expounded this teaching for the sake of sentient beings and enabled you to see Amitāyus and all in his land. Strive to do what you should. After I have passed into Nirvāṇa, do not allow doubt to arise. In the future, the Buddhist scriptures and teachings will perish." But, out of pity and compassion, I will especially preserve this sutra and maintain it in the world for a hundred years more. Those beings who encounter it will attain deliverance in accord with their aspirations.|Hisao Inagaki, translation}} Since the Jōdo-shū school was founded near the end of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, when Buddhism in Japan had become deeply involved in political schemes, and some in Japan saw monks flaunting wealth and power, it was felt that society had already reached the era of latter days of the Dharma, and that, based on the passage above, all other practices had ceased to have any efficacy. Further, Hōnen sought to provide people a simple Buddhist practice that anybody could use toward {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|qid=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}, no matter how degenerate the times because he was concerned that many people were excluded from existing sects:{{cite book |last1=Fitzgerald |first1=Joseph A. |title=Honen The Buddhist Saint: Essential Writings and Official Biography |publisher=World Wisdom |year=2006 |isbn=1-933316-13-6}} {{blockquote|"The reason I founded the Jōdo [浄土, Pure Land] sect was that I might show the ordinary man how to be born into the Buddha’s real land of recompense [e.g. the Pure Land]. According to the Tendai sect, the ordinary man may be born into the so-called Pure Land, but that land is conceived of as a very inferior place. Although the Hossō [Yogacara] sect conceives of it as indeed a very fine superior place, they do not allow that the common man can be born there at all. And all the sects, though differing in many points, all agree in not allowing that the common man can be born into the Buddha’s land of real compensation....Unless I start a separate sect, the truth that the common man may be born into the Buddha’s land of compensation will be obscured, and it will be hard to realize the deep meaning Amida [Buddha]’s Original Vow [to provide a refuge for all beings]."|Rev. Harper Coates and Rev. Ryugaku Ishizuka, translation}} Since, according to Honen's line of reasoning, salvation was mostly due to Amida Buddha's power, there was no reason why anyone who sincerely recited the ''nembutsu'' couldn't be reborn in the Pure Land. == Practice == {{JapaneseBuddhism}} Recitation of the {{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Nianfo|1=Nianfo}}, lit. {{nihongo|''namu amida butsu''|南無阿弥陀仏||"Praise to the Buddha Amitabha"}}, is the most fundamental practice of Jōdo-shū, which derives from the [[:en:Primal Vow|Primal Vow]] of Amitābha. In home practice, or in temple liturgy, the nembutsu may be recited in any number of styles including:{{cite web|title=Teachings and Practice| url=http://www.jodo.org/teachings/nembutsu.html| access-date = 2011-10-17}} * {{nihongo|'''Jūnen'''|十念||"Ten Recitations"}} - reciting the nembutsu ten times, with eight recitations of "Namu Amida Bu," followed by one "Namu Amida Butsu," and one final drawn out "Namu Amida Bu," accompanied by a bow. * {{nihongo|'''Nembutsu Ichie'''|念仏一会||"Nembutsu Gathering"}} - reciting the nembutsu as many times as possible in a sitting, regardless of number. * {{nihongo|'''Nembutsu Sanshōrai'''|念仏三唱礼||"Three Intonations of Praise"}} - a style involving three drawn-out recitations of the nembutsu, followed by a bow. This is repeated twice more for a total of nine recitations. However, in addition to this, practitioners are encouraged to engage in "auxiliary" practices, such as observing the [[:en:Five Precepts|Five Precepts]], {{ill|Buddhist meditation|en|Buddhist meditation|qid=Q1207203|lt=meditation|1=Buddhist meditation}}, the chanting of sutras, and other good conduct. There is no strict rule on this however, as Jōdo-shū stresses that the compassion of Amitābha is extended to all beings who recite the nembutsu, so how one observes auxiliary practices is left to the individual to decide. Furthermore, Jōdo-shū practitioners are allowed to worship [[kami]] and visit {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s as long as they do not worship the kami as a means to enter the pure land. [[File:Chionin01n4272.jpg|thumb|left|220px|[[Chion-in]], the highest temple of Jōdo-shū.]] Of the entire Buddhist canon, ''[[:en:Infinite Life Sutra|Sutra of Immeasurable Life]]'' is the central Buddhist scripture for Jōdo-shū Buddhism, and the foundation of the belief in the [[:en:Primal Vow|Primal Vow]] of [[Amitābha|Amida]] Buddha. In addition to this, the ''[[:en:Amitayurdhyana Sutra|Amitāyurdhyāna Sūtra]]'' and the ''[[:en:Amitabha Sutra|Amitabha Sutra]]'' are important to the Jōdo-shū school. Collectively, these are known as the {{nihongo|'''Sanbukyō'''|三部経||"Three Pure Land Sutras"}}. Further, the writings of Hōnen, contained mostly in the ''Senchaku-hongan-nembutsu-shū'' (often abbreviated to ''Senchakushū''), are another source for Jōdo-shū thought as is his last writing, the {{nihongo|''Ichimai-Kishōmon''|一枚起請文||"One-Sheet Document"}}. Most of what is known about Honen and his thought is attributed through sayings collected in the following century, the ''Senchakushū'', and letters to his students and disciples. The One-Sheet Document is also read aloud in daily services as part of Jōdo-shū liturgy. == Head Temples and Clergy == Jōdo-shū, like other Buddhist schools, maintains a professional, monastic priesthood, based on the parent {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}}-sect monastic organization, with two "head temples", one at [[Chion-in]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, and one at [[:en:Zojoji|Zojoji]] in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}. The head of the Jōdo-shū school is called the ''[[monshu]]'' in Japanese, and lives at the head temple of Chion-in. For the Seizan branch, there are three sub-branches, each with their own head temple. ==References== ==Literature== * Blum, Mark L. ''The Origins and Development of Pure Land Buddhism: a Study and Translation of Gyōnen's Jōdo Hōmon Genrushō.'' Oxford, New York, Oxford University Press, 2002. * Matsunaga, Daigan, and Alicia Matsunaga. ''Foundation of Japanese buddhism, Vol. 2: The Mass Movement (Kamakura and Muromachi Periods).'' Los Angeles, Tokyo, Buddhist Books International, 1990. * ''The Three Pure Land Sutras.'' Rev. 2nd ed. Translated by Hisao Inagaki with Harold Stewart, Numata Center for Buddhist Translation and Research, 2003. [https://www.bdk.or.jp/document/dgtl-dl/dBET_ThreePureLandSutras_2003.pdf PDF] == External links == * [http://www.jodo.org English Language Site for Jodo Shu] - The official website for Jodo Shu. Also contains information on Pure Land Buddhism in general. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20140308015357/http://www.jsri.jp/English/Main.html The Jodo Shu Research Institute] - Responsible for providing research and English-language resources on Jodo Shu, as well as publications. {{Buddhism topics}} [[en:Jōdo-shū]] [[de:Jōdo-shū]] [[es:Jōdo shū]] [[fa:فرقه جودو]] [[fr:Jōdo shū]] [[hu:Dzsódo]] [[ja:浄土宗]] [[no:Jōdo-shū]] [[pl:Jōdo-shū]] [[ro:Jōdo-shū]] [[ru:Дзёдо-сю]] [[sl:Jōdo]] [[sr:Школа чисте земље]] [[sv:Jodoshu]] [[tr:Jōdo-shū]] [[uk:Секта Дзьодо]] [[wuu:淨土宗]] [[zh:净土宗 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q912208}} [[Category:1175 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:12th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Jōdo-shū| ]] [[Category:Pure Land Buddhism]] [[Category:Schools of Buddhism founded in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
